sections.title: Regular -en Verbs
sections.explanation: Most German verbs end in -en in the infinitive (machen, wohnen, spielen). To conjugate in the present tense, remove -en to get the stem, then add personal endings: ich -e, du -st, er/sie/es -t, wir -en, ihr -t, sie/Sie -en. Example: machen (to make/do) → Stem: mach- → ich mache, du machst, er macht, wir machen, ihr macht, sie machen. If the stem ends in -t or -d, add an extra 'e' for pronunciation: du arbeitest (not 'arbeitst').
sections.tip: Ending pattern: ich -e, du -st, er/sie/es -t, wir -en, ihr -t, sie/Sie -en. The wir and sie/Sie forms match the infinitive!

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'spielen' (to play) for 'er'?
exercises.explanation: 3rd person singular (er/sie/es) takes the -t ending: spiel + t = spielt.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'wohnen' (to live) for 'du'?
exercises.explanation: 2nd person singular (du) takes the -st ending: wohn + st = wohnst.

exercises.prompt: Wir ___ (spielen) Fußball. (We play football.)
exercises.explanation: wir + spielen: the wir-form keeps the full -en ending. Wir spielen Fußball.

exercises.prompt: Why is 'du arbeitest' correct (not 'du arbeitst')?
options: The stem ends in -t, so an extra -e- is added for pronunciation
exercises.explanation: When the stem ends in -t or -d (arbeit-, red-), an extra -e- is inserted before the ending: du arbeitest, er arbeitet.

exercises.prompt: Match each pronoun to the ending added to the verb stem:
exercises.explanation: The standard present tense endings: ich -e, du -st, er/sie/es -t, wir -en, ihr -t, sie/Sie -en.

exercises.prompt: Conjugate 'lernen' (to learn) for 'ich':
exercises.explanation: 1st person singular (ich) takes -e: lern + e = lerne. Ich lerne Deutsch.

exercises.prompt: Ihr ___ (kommen) heute. You all come today. — use ihr form
exercises.explanation: ihr + kommen: komm + t = kommt. Ihr kommt heute.

exercises.prompt: Which two pronoun forms use the -en ending?
exercises.explanation: wir and sie/Sie both take the -en ending, which is identical to the infinitive: wir machen, sie machen, Sie machen.

sections.title: Irregular: sein and haben
sections.explanation: The two most important German verbs are 'sein' (to be) and 'haben' (to have). Both are highly irregular and must be memorized. sein: ich bin, du bist, er/sie/es ist, wir sind, ihr seid, sie/Sie sind. haben: ich habe, du hast, er/sie/es hat, wir haben, ihr habt, sie/Sie haben. These verbs are used constantly in everyday speech and as auxiliary verbs for the perfect tense.
sections.tip: Memorize 'sein' first: bin, bist, ist, sind, seid, sind. It's the most-used verb in the language!

examples.en: I am hungry. (lit: I have hunger)

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'sein' for 'ich'?
exercises.explanation: 'sein' (to be): ich bin. This is completely irregular — unlike the stem-based pattern for regular verbs.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'sein' for 'wir'?
exercises.explanation: 'sein': wir sind. Note: 'seid' is for 'ihr' (you all).

exercises.prompt: Er ___ (haben) ein Fahrrad. (He has a bicycle.)
exercises.explanation: 'haben' for er/sie/es is 'hat': Er hat ein Fahrrad.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I am hungry' in German? (German uses 'haben')
exercises.explanation: German says 'Ich habe Hunger' (I have hunger) rather than 'I am hungry'. Similarly: Ich habe Durst (I am thirsty).

exercises.prompt: Match each pronoun to the correct form of 'sein':
exercises.explanation: sein: ich bin, du bist, er/sie/es ist, wir sind, ihr seid, sie/Sie sind.

exercises.prompt: What is the form of 'haben' for 'ihr'?
exercises.explanation: 'haben' for ihr: habt. Note the -t ending for ihr (same as regular verbs), but 'hab-' loses the 'e' of the stem.

exercises.prompt: Wir ___ (sein) Studenten. We are students.
exercises.explanation: 'sein': wir sind. Wir sind Studenten = We are students.

exercises.prompt: Which form of 'haben' is used for 'sie' (she)?
exercises.explanation: 'haben' for er/sie/es is 'hat'. Sie hat = She has.

exercises.prompt: Choose the correct sentence: 'You (informal) are from Germany.'
exercises.explanation: 'du' + sein = bist. 'Du bist aus Deutschland' uses the correct form and the preposition 'aus' (from, out of).

sections.title: Strong Verbs: Stem Vowel Changes
sections.explanation: Many common German verbs change their stem vowel in the du and er/sie/es forms. The most common changes are: e → i (sprechen→sprichst, helfen→hilfst), e → ie (lesen→liest, sehen→siehst), a → ä (fahren→fährst, schlafen→schläfst). These are called 'strong verbs'. The ich, wir, ihr, and sie forms use the unchanged stem. Only du and er/sie/es change. You must learn which verbs do this.
sections.tip: Only du and er/sie/es forms change in strong verbs. ich, wir, and sie keep the original stem vowel.

examples.de: sprechen: ich spreche, du sprichst, er spricht
examples.en: to speak: e→i change in du/er forms

examples.de: lesen: ich lese, du liest, er liest
examples.en: to read: e→ie change in du/er forms

examples.de: fahren: ich fahre, du fährst, er fährt
examples.en: to drive: a→ä change in du/er forms

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'sprechen' (to speak) for 'er'?
exercises.explanation: 'sprechen' has an e→i stem change in the er/sie/es form: sprich + t = spricht.

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'lesen' (to read) for 'du'?
exercises.explanation: 'lesen' has an e→ie stem change in the du and er forms: liest (not 'lesst').

exercises.prompt: Er ___ (fahren) schnell. He drives fast. — strong verb a→ä
exercises.hint: a changes to ä in er/sie/es
exercises.explanation: 'fahren' has an a→ä change: er fährt (not 'fahrt'). Only du and er/sie/es forms change.

exercises.prompt: Which forms of a strong verb keep the original stem vowel?
exercises.explanation: Only 'du' and 'er/sie/es' show the vowel change. ich, wir, ihr, sie/Sie keep the original vowel.

exercises.prompt: Match each verb to its er/sie/es form:
exercises.explanation: sprechen→spricht (e→i), lesen→liest (e→ie), fahren→fährt (a→ä), sehen→sieht (e→ie).

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form of 'helfen' (to help) for 'du'?
exercises.explanation: 'helfen' has an e→i change: hilf + st = hilfst.
